description.purpose: diagnostic_uncertainty — 63-year-old male with I've been having increased thirst or urination recently for a few weeks
description.clinical_scenario: Patient presents with I've been having increased thirst or urination recently for a few weeks. Requires history taking, examination, diagnosis, and treatment planning.
educational_objectives: Ability to probe for hidden symptoms
description.focus: Systematic information gathering and differential diagnosis

user_scenario.persona: 63-year-old male patient, 小学 education, works as a factory worker. a patient who is confused about medical terms and needs simple explanations presenting with I've been having increased thirst or urination recently.

patient_profile.chief_complaint: heart disease for a few weeks

patient_knows.general_concern: I've been having increased thirst or urination recently
patient_knows.family_history: family member has high blood pressure

insulin_fears: I heard insulin is addictive — once you start, you can never stop
My neighbor's aunt took insulin and got so thin, it's scary
medication_concerns: Long-term diabetes medication will ruin my kidneys
Western medicine has too many side effects, can I take Chinese herbs instead?
Can I just control it with diet instead of medication?
dietary_extremes: Does having diabetes mean I can never eat anything sweet?
Do I have to only eat whole grains and never eat rice again?
prognosis_concerns: Will I go blind or lose my legs soon?
How many years do I have left?
economic_concerns: How much will this medication cost per month?
Can we only prescribe what's absolutely necessary?

share_only_if_asked: kidney disease (needs doctor to specifically ask)
hidden_truth: high blood pressure (won't mention unless specifically probed)
my appetite has changed (won't mention unless specifically probed)
information_sharing_strategy.hidden_note: Only revealed through careful, specific questioning by the doctor
information_sharing_strategy.noise_note: Unrelated symptoms that may distract from the main issue
misleading_symptoms: i've been having lost weight without trying
recurrent skin infections and slow wound healing
unexplained weight loss (also seen in hyperthyroidism and cancer)
persistent fatigue (also seen in anemia and depression)
information_sharing_strategy.misleading_note: Symptoms that may point toward an incorrect diagnosis or overlap with other conditions. Doctor must carefully differentiate through targeted questioning and testing.

progressive_reveal.trigger: doctor asks a specific follow-up question about related symptoms
doctor asks a specific follow-up question about related symptoms
doctor asks a specific follow-up question about related symptoms

share_in_response_to_medication_discussi: Concerns about metformin side effects — severe kidney disease

user_scenario.task_instructions: You are LiXX, a 63-year-old male patient. Your education level is 小学. You work as a factory worker. You are seeing a doctor because of heart disease. This has been going on for about a few weeks. You don't understand medical terms. Express your symptoms in your own simple words. Ask for clarification if the doctor uses terms you don't understand. You've been reading about your symptoms online and you're worried it might be something serious. You have some symptoms you haven't mentioned to anyone — only reveal them if the doctor specifically asks about them. You are sensitive about medical costs. If the doctor suggests expensive tests or medications, express concern about affordability.

lifestyle_factors.smoking: quit 2 years ago (former 1 pack/day for 25 years)

hypertension.interaction_with_primary: may worsen fatigue, confusing with disease-related tiredness

chronic kidney disease.interaction_with_primary: may worsen fatigue, confusing with disease-related tiredness

coronary artery disease.interaction_with_primary: may worsen fatigue, confusing with disease-related tiredness

communication_checks.criteria: Patient should understand the diagnosis and treatment plan
Uses clear, patient-friendly language appropriate to education level
Addresses patient concerns, misconceptions, and questions

chief_complaint_detail.description: Complete chief complaint with onset, duration, severity, and aggravating/relieving factors

symptoms_completeness.description: All major symptoms of type 2 diabetes identified through systematic questioning

past_medical_history.description: Relevant past medical history including comorbidities

current_medications.description: Current medication list with doses and adherence

must_ask: Have you noticed increased thirst or urination recently?
Have you had any episodes of blurred vision?

treatment_scenarios.description: Patient reports side effects from metformin (biguanide)
options: Add adjunct medication to manage side effects
treatment_scenarios.correct_approach: Start with low-dose metformin, titrate up, monitor symptoms; consider alternative if intolerant

drug_interaction_check.description: Must check drug interactions before prescribing

kidney_function_before_medication.description: Must assess: kidney function before medication

kidney_function_before_metformin.description: Must assess kidney function before prescribing metformin

forbidden_actions: Agree with patient misconceptions without correction
Ignore abnormal lab values without explanation or follow-up plan
Diagnose without asking about symptom onset, duration, and progression
Diagnose without exploring at least one differential diagnosis
Dismiss patient concerns without addressing them
Use medical jargon without explanation when patient has limited health literacy
Fail to correct patient misconceptions about treatment
Prescribe contraindicated drug without documenting rationale
Order unnecessary tests without clinical justification
Prescribe metformin without checking kidney function (eGFR)
Ignore declining kidney function in medication dosing
Fail to consider Metabolic Syndrome as alternative diagnosis
Prescribe metformin in patient with severe kidney disease without special precautions
Diagnose type 2 diabetes without ordering any supporting lab tests
Fail to assess comorbidity impact on treatment plan
Prescribe multiple new medications simultaneously without interaction check

uncertainty_events.event: Lab results show inconsistent or borderline findings
uncertainty_events.example: borderline HbA1c vs high fasting glucose
uncertainty_events.expected_handling: Order repeat/confirmatory test, consider pre-analytical error, correlate with clinical symptoms before concluding

uncertainty_events.event: Patient casually mentions 'high blood pressure' when discussing treatment options
uncertainty_events.example: 'Oh, I also sometimes get high blood pressure — is that related?'
uncertainty_events.expected_handling: Recognize this may indicate undiagnosed high blood pressure, update differential, order appropriate workup

uncertainty_events.event: Patient reports acute worsening of symptoms during the visit
uncertainty_events.example: Patient becomes visibly more uncomfortable or reports new severe symptom
uncertainty_events.expected_handling: Reassess urgency, consider acute complication, adjust pace and priorities of the consultation
